title: Domain Model Extraction
use_for: extracting domain concepts, rules, inputs, outputs, and invariants from an existing implementation
turning old behavior into a neutral domain model before a rewrite
separating product intent from old architecture and infrastructure
building a capability map for new design work
do_not_use_for: low-level infrastructure design without domain content
rules: name domain concepts without using project-specific identifiers
separate domain rules from UI, persistence, lifecycle, and external API mechanics
record uncertainty explicitly instead of inventing missing requirements
classify evidence by confidence and source
prefer capability language over old file or class names
procedure: For each capability, identify inputs, state, decisions, outputs, side effects, and failure modes.
Separate domain rules from infrastructure mechanisms.
Mark rules as confirmed, inferred, obsolete, conflicting, or unknown.
Define the smallest neutral model that explains the desired behavior.
Feed the model into architecture, pipeline, UI, and validation planning.

primary_when: the task is primarily about deriving a clean domain model from old code or documents
must_not_own_when: the task is primarily runtime lifecycle or build-system work

solves: how to preserve domain knowledge without preserving old architecture
how to make requirements explicit before a rewrite
how to distinguish domain rules from implementation accidents

core_principles: Domain intent is more important than legacy shape during a rewrite.
A useful domain model explains decisions, state, and side effects in neutral language.
constraints: Do not include project names, local paths, or concrete class names in reusable guidance.
Do not treat UI labels or config keys as domain concepts without confirmation.
Do not erase conflicting evidence; record it for design resolution.
decision_rules: If behavior affects user-visible or domain-visible outcomes, capture it as domain evidence.
If behavior only compensates for infrastructure limitations, classify it as implementation evidence.
If two old paths conflict, mark the rule unresolved until validated.
If a rule cannot be explained without old class names, rename it in capability language.

avoid_patterns: old folder structure as domain model
copying class names into new architecture
treating infrastructure workarounds as domain rules

recipes.name: Extract domain intent from old code
recipes.when: Use when old implementation is being archived but its product meaning must be preserved.
steps: Map old evidence to inputs, rules, state, outputs, and side effects.
Separate domain rules from infrastructure mechanisms.
Use the resulting model as input for new architecture.

pitfalls: Mistaking old abstractions for domain concepts.
Losing edge cases because they are hidden inside infrastructure code.
Leaking named-project vocabulary into reusable guidance.
review_checklist: Are capabilities named without project-specific terms?
Are domain rules separated from infrastructure mechanisms?
Are side effects and failure modes captured?
Can the model guide new design without copying old code?

references.title: Contracts
references.interpretation: Use to express extracted domain rules as explicit contracts.
references.why: Domain extraction needs stable rules and expectations before new architecture is designed.
